verses.text: These are 'the last words of David,' and he names their source: 'The Spirit of the LORD spake by me, and his word was in my tongue.' The sweet psalmist of Israel knew his songs were not his own. The truest words a man can leave are those God puts in his mouth.

These are 'the last words of David,' and he names their source: 'The Spirit of the LORD spake by me, and his word was in my tongue.' The sweet psalmist of Israel knew his songs were not his own. The truest words a man can leave are those God puts in his mouth.

David sings of the ideal ruler: 'he that ruleth over men must be just, ruling in the fear of God,' and such a reign is like 'the light of the morning' after rain. Just, God-fearing leadership is a sunrise to a people — and it points to the perfect King yet to come.

David sings of the ideal ruler: 'he that ruleth over men must be just, ruling in the fear of God,' and such a reign is like 'the light of the morning' after rain. Just, God-fearing leadership is a sunrise to a people — and it points to the perfect King yet to come.

David confesses his house is not all it should be, yet rests on this: 'he hath made with me an everlasting covenant, ordered in all things, and sure.' His hope is not in his own record but in God's unbreakable promise. Sure salvation stands on God's covenant, not our worthiness.

The wicked, by contrast, are like thorns thrust away, not taken by hand but with iron, and burned. A life set against God ends in the fire it gathered. The same word that comforts the covenant heir is a warning to the rebel: thorns are for burning.

The wicked, by contrast, are like thorns thrust away, not taken by hand but with iron, and burned. A life set against God ends in the fire it gathered. The same word that comforts the covenant heir is a warning to the rebel: thorns are for burning.

The roll of David's mighty men begins: Adino, who slew eight hundred at once; Eleazar, who fought till his hand froze to the sword and 'the LORD wrought a great victory.' Courage that holds its ground till the hand cramps is honoured — yet the victory is God's.

The roll of David's mighty men begins: Adino, who slew eight hundred at once; Eleazar, who fought till his hand froze to the sword and 'the LORD wrought a great victory.' Courage that holds its ground till the hand cramps is honoured — yet the victory is God's.

The roll of David's mighty men begins: Adino, who slew eight hundred at once; Eleazar, who fought till his hand froze to the sword and 'the LORD wrought a great victory.' Courage that holds its ground till the hand cramps is honoured — yet the victory is God's.

Shammah stood alone in a field of lentils when all others fled, defended it, and 'the LORD wrought a great victory.' He would not give up a patch of ground that seemed not worth saving. Faithfulness holds the small post others abandon, and God makes the stand count.

Shammah stood alone in a field of lentils when all others fled, defended it, and 'the LORD wrought a great victory.' He would not give up a patch of ground that seemed not worth saving. Faithfulness holds the small post others abandon, and God makes the stand count.

Three break through enemy lines to fetch David water from Bethlehem's well, but he pours it out to the LORD, unwilling to drink what was won at the risk of their blood. He treats their devotion as too sacred to spend on himself. Love poured out deserves to be offered up to God.

Abishai, chief of the second rank, lifted his spear against three hundred and won a name among the mighty. Great courage earns honour, even when it falls short of the very first rank. There is esteem for all who serve valiantly, each in his own measure.

Abishai, chief of the second rank, lifted his spear against three hundred and won a name among the mighty. Great courage earns honour, even when it falls short of the very first rank. There is esteem for all who serve valiantly, each in his own measure.

Benaiah killed two lion-like men of Moab, slew a lion in a pit on a snowy day, and felled a giant Egyptian with his own spear. He acted bravely in the worst conditions, and David set him over his guard. God rewards the courage that does not wait for easy circumstances.

The long roll of the thirty begins — Asahel, Elhanan, Shammah, Helez, and more, each named, each from his town. These are not the famous three, yet every name is written down. God keeps the record of His faithful, even the ones history would otherwise forget.

The long roll of the thirty begins — Asahel, Elhanan, Shammah, Helez, and more, each named, each from his town. These are not the famous three, yet every name is written down. God keeps the record of His faithful, even the ones history would otherwise forget.

The list goes on — Heleb, Ittai, Benaiah, Hiddai, and others gathered from across Israel. Ordinary men of valour, remembered one by one. In the LORD's book no faithful servant is anonymous; the long catalogue itself is an honour, name beside name.

The list goes on — Heleb, Ittai, Benaiah, Hiddai, and others gathered from across Israel. Ordinary men of valour, remembered one by one. In the LORD's book no faithful servant is anonymous; the long catalogue itself is an honour, name beside name.

The list goes on — Heleb, Ittai, Benaiah, Hiddai, and others gathered from across Israel. Ordinary men of valour, remembered one by one. In the LORD's book no faithful servant is anonymous; the long catalogue itself is an honour, name beside name.

More names follow — Shammah, Ahiam, Eliphelet, Eliam, Igal, Bani — warriors who gave their strength to David's cause. They asked no monument; their place in the king's roll is enough. To be counted among the servants of God's anointed is honour enough for any life.

More names follow — Shammah, Ahiam, Eliphelet, Eliam, Igal, Bani — warriors who gave their strength to David's cause. They asked no monument; their place in the king's roll is enough. To be counted among the servants of God's anointed is honour enough for any life.

More names follow — Shammah, Ahiam, Eliphelet, Eliam, Igal, Bani — warriors who gave their strength to David's cause. They asked no monument; their place in the king's roll is enough. To be counted among the servants of God's anointed is honour enough for any life.

The roll closes with Zelek, Naharai, Ira, Gareb — and last, 'Uriah the Hittite: thirty and seven in all.' That final name falls like a shadow: the loyal man David wronged. Scripture honours him to the end, a quiet reminder that even great David was a sinner saved by grace.
